title: Calculations of a Plane Turbulent Jet
text:
Abstract 
A conditionally-modelled joint pdf transport equa- 
tion has been solved to calculate the properties of 
the self-similar, plane, turbulent jet. The joint 
probability density function (pdf) is that of the 
three velocity components and of the nozzle-f h i d  
concentration, which is a conserved passive sca- 
lar. In the pdf transport equation, convective 
transport does not have to be modelled, while the 
effects of dissipation and of the fluctuating pres- 
sure gradient are modelled conditional upon the 
state of the fluid - 
turbulent or irrotational. 
The calculated intermittency factor, and condition- 
al velocities and. Reynolds stresses are compared 
with the available experimental data.

Transport equations for pdf 's are useful in 
modelling turbulent flows because non-linear one- 
point processes (such as convection and reaction) 
can be treated without approximation1 
r 4 r  5 .  
The 
transport equation for the joint pdf of a set of 
scalars (e.g. compositions) is particularly useful 
in reactive flows because the term pertaining to 
reaction appears in closed form, irrespective of 
the complexit and non-linearity of the reaction 
scheme. Popez and Janicka, Kolbe and ~ollmann' 
have reported accurate calculations of premixed and 
diffusion flames based on the scalar joint pdf 
equation. 
The joint pdf of several scalars or the joint 
pdf of velocity and scalars is a function of many 
independent variables. (The joint pdf considered 
here £(I, $; 8) is a function of five independent 
variables. ) Because of this large dimensionality, 
it is impracticable to obtain solutions to the 
joint pdf equations by f inite-dif ference methods: 
but Monte Carlo m e t h ~ d s ~ ~ ~ h a v e  
been developed 
that allow the equations to be solved for the gen- 
eral case, without excessive demands on computer 
time or storage. The calculations reported here 
required 7 minutes CPU time on an IBM 3701168. 
The first experimental study of intermittency 
in free shear flows was performed in 1949 by Town- 
send1' ,which was followed by the detailed investi- 
gation of Corrsin and Kistler ''in 
1955. In the 
last fifteen years, many experiments have been per- 
formed on free shear flows and boundary layers in 
which the intermittency and conditional statistics 
have been measured: these experiments are reviewed 
by ~ntonia". 
Since the turbulent fluid is highly 
rotational while the nonturbulent fluid is irrota- 
tional, the fluid behavior can be expected to be 
significantly different in these two states. The 
experiments confirm this expectation. 
It can also be expected that a model that ex- 
plicitly accounts for the different behavior of 
turbulent and non-turbulent fluid can be more 
accurate than one that makes no distinction.

Since then there have been s e v e r a l  works [e.g. 
r e f s .  15-18] a l o y j  s i m i l a r  l i n e s  
Recently, Koll- 
mann and Janicka 
and ~ o l l m a n n l '  have modelled t h e  
transport eequation f o r  the pdf of a s i n g l e  conser- 
ved s c a l a r  taking intermittency i n t o  account. 
The 
modelled s c a l a r  pdf equation was solved by a f i n -  
i t e - d i f  f ereace technique t o  c a l c u l a t e  the i n t e r m i t  - 
tency f a c t o r  and conditional s t a t i s t i c s  i n  two 
plane shear flows. 
I n  t h i s  work it i s  t h e  j o i n t  
pdf of velocity and a conserved s c a l a r  t h a t  is con- 
s i d e r e d ,  and t h e  conditionally-modelled t r a n s p o r t  
equation is solved by a Monte Carlo method. 
I n  t h e  next s e c t i o n  the j o i n t  pdf equation and 
its unconditional modelling a r e  described. 
The 
following t h r e e  s e c t i o n s  then describe the condi- 
t i o n a l  modelling, the s o l u t i o n  procedure, and t h e

j e t .

Summary and Conclusion 
A conditionally-modelled j o i n t  pdf equation 
has been solved by a Monte Carlo method t o  calcu- 
l a t e  the one-point s t a t i s t i c a l  p r o p e r t i e s  of a 
self-similar plane turbulent j e t .  
I n  t h e  j o i n t  pdf 
transport equation, convective t r a n s p o r t  appears i n  
closed form, and s o  gradient-diffusion modelling is 
avoided. 
Within t h e  turbulent f l u i d ,  a s t o c h a s t i c  
mixing model and a s t o c h a s t i c  r e o r i e n t a t i o n  model 
a r e  used t o  model t h e  e f f e c t s  of d i s s i p a t i o n  and 
the f l u c t u a t i n g  pressure gradient. 
Within t h e  non- 
-turbulent f l u i d  t h e r e  is no d i s s i p a t i o n ,  and i t  is 
assumed t h a t  the velocity f l u c t u a t i o n s  a r e  i s o t r o -  
p i c  with a j o i n t  normal pdf. 
The modelling of t h e  i n t e r a c t i o n s  between tur- 
bulent and non-turbulent f l u i d  c e n t e r s  on t h e  r a i e  
of entrainment g, t h e  r a t e  of mom$ntum e ~ c h a n g e  5, 
and the r a t e s  of energy t r a n s f e r  ET and EN: 
models f o r  these processes a r e  given by equations 
(39), (42), (45) and (47).
